coredata.dc:title: Copper nanoclusters stabilized by D-penicillamine for ultrasensitive and visual detection of oxytetracycline
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tengfei
Liu, Wei
Tian, Shiyao
Tian, Dating
coredata.dc:description: Copper nanoclusters (DPA@CuNCs) with red fluorescence were successfully synthesized by a one-step method based on D-penicillamine (DPA), which acted not only as a reducing agent but also as a stabilizer. The products were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, particle-size analysis, ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry, and fluorescence spectrometry. When the excitation wavelength was 280 nm, DPA@CuNCs emitted bright red fluorescence at 640 nm with a fluorescence quantum yield of 5.8 %. Due to the inner filter effect, oxytetracycline (OTC) effectively quenched the fluorescence of DPA@CuNCs, and then DPA@CuNCs were applied to the trace detection of OTC. The method showed a good linear range for OTC from 5 to 60 μmol/L, with a detection limit of 0.026 μmol/L and a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9983. Moreover, a paper-based sensor for the visual detection of OTC has been developed, which can conveniently and rapidly distinguish the concentration ranges of OTC through the color changes of the test papers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Oxytetracycline
Visual paper-based sensor
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of OTC in real samples
Detection of OTC and quenching mechanism
Detection of OTC in real samples and comparison of this method with other methods
Fabrication of paper-based sensor for visual detection of OTC

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51263009).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra, (B) XPS spectrum, (C) Cu 2p spectrum, and (D) TEM images and the particle-size distribution of the samples.

(A) Images of the stock solution under the sunlight (1) and the ultraviolet light (2), and images of the diluted solution under the natural light (3) and the ultraviolet light (4). (B) Excitation and emission spectra of the samples. (C) Emission spectra of the samples under different excitation wavelengths. (D) Emission spectra of the samples prepared with different ratios of DPA to Cu(NO3)2·3H2O. (E) Emission spectra of the samples under different reaction temperatures. (F) Emission spectra of the samples under different reaction times.

(A) Emission spectra of the samples in the presence of OTC. (B) The linear plot of the F0-F values versus the OTC concentrations. (C) UV–vis spectra of the samples in the presence of OTC, and OTC. (D) Fluorescence lifetime of the samples.

Influence of interference ions (A) and organic compounds (B) on the F0-F values of DPA@CuNCs (Fig. 4A from 1 to 12: K+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Eu3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and OTC; Fig. 4B from 1 to 12: streptomycin, sodium artemisinin, sodium penicillin, lincomycin hydrochloride, atropine citrulline, glutamate, ascorbic acid, cysteine, hemoglobin, and OTC).
(A) Photos of test papers stored in the dark for different days. (B) Photos of test papers loaded with different OTC concentrations. (a, under the sunlight; b, under the ultraviolet).
Detection results of OTC in swine wastewater and pig kidney extracts.

head.ce:title: Copper nanoclusters stabilized by D-penicillamine for ultrasensitive and visual detection of oxytetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Hubei Key Laboratory of Biological Resources Protection and Utilization, Hubei Minzu University, Enshi 445000, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering
Hubei Key Laboratory of Biological Resources Protection and Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Hubei Key Laboratory of Biological Resources Protection and Utilization, Hubei Minzu University, Enshi 445000, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Xuyuan Road, Enshi 445000, People’s Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper nanoclusters (DPA@CuNCs) with red fluorescence were successfully synthesized by a one-step method based on D-penicillamine (DPA), which acted not only as a reducing agent but also as a stabilizer. The products were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, particle-size analysis, ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry, and fluorescence spectrometry. When the excitation wavelength was 280 nm, DPA@CuNCs emitted bright red fluorescence at 640 nm with a fluorescence quantum yield of 5.8 %. Due to the inner filter effect, oxytetracycline (OTC) effectively quenched the fluorescence of DPA@CuNCs, and then DPA@CuNCs were applied to the trace detection of OTC. The method showed a good linear range for OTC from 5 to 60 μmol/L, with a detection limit of 0.026 μmol/L and a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9983. Moreover, a paper-based sensor for the visual detection of OTC has been developed, which can conveniently and rapidly distinguish the concentration ranges of OTC through the color changes of the test papers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, with the rapid development of the social economy, plenty of antibiotics are consumed every year around the world [1]. These antibiotics are widely used in animal husbandry, aquaculture, and medical care [2,3], which cannot be fully absorbed into the organism, and the majority are excreted unchanged in feces and urine to the environment [4]. The decomposition of antibiotics by natural degradation requires a long degradation cycle and a complex degradation process, which leads to the enrichment of antibiotics in the environment, seriously affecting the ecological environment and human health [5]. Therefore, how to achieve the effective detection of antibiotics in the environment has become a topic of concern to researchers.

Oxytetracycline (OTC) is a broad-spectrum antibiotic. It has a good antibacterial effect on Gram-positive bacteria, Rickettsia, E. coli, etc., and thus has a good curative effect on typhoid, anthrax, dysentery of livestock and poultry, especially on swine pneumonia. Therefore, OTC is widely used in livestock and poultry feed additives, resulting in its abuse, which seriously affects the ecological environment. Various methods have been used to detect OTC in the environment, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], spectrophotometry [7], colorimetry [8], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [9], electrochemical sensors [10], Raman spectrophotometry [11], fluorescence [12], etc. Among these methods, the fluorescence method has attracted much attention due to its good selectivity, simple operation, and high sensitivity.

Compared with traditional fluorescent dyes, fluorescent metal nanoclusters (MNCs) have the advantages of convenient preparation, large Stokes shift, spectral tunability, photostability, and biocompatibility [13,14], which make them good application prospects in biological imaging, fluorescent labeling, environmental monitoring, and other fields [15–17]. In general, we should select appropriate ligands during the synthesis of MNCs. These ligands not only act as stabilizers to protect MNCs but also affect the fluorescence properties of MNCs.

In general, appropriate ligands should be selected in the synthesis of MNCs because these ligands affect the fluorescence properties of MNCs. Common ligands include thiol compounds, proteins, and polymers [12,18–20], some of which can act as both reducing agents and protective agents. In the synthesis process, such ligands only need to react with metal precursors by one step to obtain corresponding MNCs, thus avoiding the addition of other substances. Because the one-step method is simple, fast, and does not require complicated post-processing, it attracts much attention. For example, cysteine [21], glutathione [22], and dimercaptosuccinic acid [23] have been used as ligands to prepare MNCs by the one-step method.

In recent years, many reports on gold and silver nanoclusters (NCs) have been published [24]. However, there is relatively little research on copper nanoclusters (CuNCs). Compared with gold and silver NCs, CuNCs also show good fluorescence performance, especially at a relatively low cost. However, CuNCs are easily oxidized, making the synthesis process of CuNCs relatively cumbersome and complex [25]. Ouyang and coworkers [26] synthesized CuNCs using DNA as a template. However, the fluorescence intensity of the products decreased significantly after 165 min. Kawasaki et al [27] prepared CuNCs under harsh conditions of strong alkali and high temperature using ethylene glycol as ligands. Guo et al [28] synthesized CuNCs by using trypsin as a template molecule and hydrazine hydrate as a reducing agent in a high-temperature environment for 4 h. Therefore, it is necessary to explore a simple and convenient synthesis method for CuNCs with good stability and mild reaction conditions. As is well known, D-penicillamine (DPA) contains not only amino and carboxyl groups but also sulfhydryl groups. The sulfhydryl group endows DPA with good reducibility [29], which makes it possible to reduce Cu2+ to Cu0. Moreover, the amino and carboxyl groups can stabilize metal nanoparticles [30]. Therefore, DPA can be used as a suitable capping ligand in the synthesis of CuNCs.

In this work, DPA@CuNCs were prepared using copper nitrate as the substrate and D-penicillamine (DPA) as the ligand, where DPA is not only a protective agent but also a reducing agent. The structures of the products were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), particle-size analysis, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The fluorescence characteristics of the products were investigated, and a fluorescent sensor of DPA@CuNCs for rapid, simple, and convenient detection of OTC was designed, based on the fluorescence inner filter effect (IFE). Moreover, the fluorescence sensor has been successfully applied to the detection of OTC in practical samples, such as pig wastewater and pig kidney extracts. Finally, a DPA@CuNCs paper for visual detection of OTC was developed, thus realizing semi-quantitative detection of OTC by the naked eye.

Cu(NO3)2·3H2O,(99 %), D-penicillamine (DPA, 99 %), oxytetracycline (98 %), and other chemicals were all of the analytical grade and all purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., ltd(Shanghai, China). The water used in the work was secondary distilled water.

The fluorescence experiments were performed on a Hitachi F-4600 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan), with 5.0/5.0 nm of the EX/ EM slit width and 1200 nm of the scanning speed. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were measured on an ESCALAB 250 XI instrument (UK). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis was performed on a Thermo Nicolet 370 spectrometer (USA), with 4000–400 cm−1 as the measurement range. The sample morphology was observed by a JOEL JEM 2100-F transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tokyo, Japan) with 200-kV accelerating voltage. The absorption experiments were performed on a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). The fluorescence lifetime was measured on an FLS 980 steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The particle-size was measured on a Zetasizer nano analyzer. The pH value was measured on a PHS-3E pH meter (Shanghai, China).

DPA@CuNCs were prepared by the one-step method using DPA as both a reducing agent and a protective agent. First, copper nitrate solution (0.1 mL, 0.100 mol/L) was added to 0.5 mL of DPA (0.100 mol/L) at room temperature. Then, the solution was sonicated for 30 min to obtain a light-yellow mixture. Finally, the mixture was purified with a dialysis membrane (MW: 2000 Da) for 12 h and then stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before further use.

First, a series of mixed solutions of DPA@CuNCs and PBS (pH = 5; prepared by adding NaOH solution to a certain amount of NaH2PO4 solution with 0.2 mol/L [31]) were formulated. Then, different concentrations of OTC were added to the above-mixed solution. After full incubation, 2 mL of the mixed solution was sucked into the quartz cuvette, and then the fluorescence intensities were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (the excitation wavelength was 280 nm).

For the specific selection experiment of DPA@CuNCs towards OTC, different interferents were added to the mixture solution. The interferents contain K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Eu3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, streptomycin, artemisinin sodium, penicillin sodium, lincomycin hydrochloride, atropine, citrulline, glutamic acid, ascorbic acid, cysteine, hemoglobin, and oxytetracycline. The fluorescent data were obtained under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The detection of OTC in swine wastewater and pig kidney was investigated. The swine wastewater came from a pig farm in Hubei Province, China. Before detection, the swine wastewater was centrifuged at 12000 rad/min for 10 min and then filtered by a filter membrane. The filtrate was used to prepare the OTC solution. Specifically, OTC standard solutions with different concentrations were spiked into the mixture solution of the filtrate and the DPA@CuNCs, and then the fluorescence data were measured. The concentration of OTC was calculated by the linear regression equation. The recovery was determined according to Equation (1) [32], and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained through three parallel experiments under the same conditions.
(1)
Recovery =
Measuredconcentration
Spikedconcentration
× 100 %

Moreover, pig kidneys were purchased from the local market. After the pig kidney was washed, cut into pieces, crushed, immersed in a methanol solution, and sonicated for 20 min, the supernatant was collected by centrifugation. The above operations were repeated three times. The obtained supernatants were concentrated with a rotary evaporator and then filtered with a filter membrane. The filtrate was used to prepare the OTC solution. Then, the detection of OTC was carried out following the above procedures.

The quantum yield (QY) of DPA@CuNCs was obtained according to the method provided in the previously published paper [25]. The data were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 280 nm and a slit width of 5/5 nm. The QY value is calculated according to Equation (2) [33].
(2)
QY =
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2
× Q
Y R
where I represents the integral area of the fluorescence spectrum, η is the refractive index of the solvent, A is the ultraviolet–visible absorption at the excitation wavelength of 280 nm, and R is the reference value.

FTIR is one of the important means to measure whether the product is successfully prepared. Fig. 1 A shows the FTIR spectra of DPA (a) and DPA@CuNCs (b). As shown in Fig. 1A-a, the absorption peak caused by the symmetric stretching of COO– in DPA appeared at 1384 cm−1, and the absorption peak caused by the stretching vibration of the SH bond appeared at 2516 cm−1 [34]. Fig. 1A-b shows the FTIR spectrum of DPA@CuNCs. As shown in Fig. 1A-b, the absorption peaks caused by NH stretching vibration and bending vibration appeared at 3432 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1, respectively. However, compared with Fig. 1A-a, the characteristic peak of the SH bond at 2516 cm−1 completely disappeared in Fig. 1A-b, indicating that the SH bond was broken and the SCu bond was formed [35].

Fig. 1B and C show the XPS of DPA@CuNCs (B) and Cu 2p spectrum of DPA@CuNCs (C). As shown in Fig. 1B, the peaks appeared at 163.08 eV, 285.08 eV, 401.08 eV, 531.08 eV, and 932.08 eV, corresponding to S, C, N, O, and Cu elements in the DPA@CuNCs, respectively. Moreover, the atomic percentages of each atom in DPA-CuNCs were shown in the Supplementary Information (Table S1). As shown in Fig. 1C, two peaks were observed at 932.26 eV and 951.94 eV, which were caused by 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 ionization of Cu (0)/Cu (I). And there was no characteristic peak of Cu2+ at 942 eV, indicating that Cu2+ has been reduced to Cu (0) and Cu (I) [36]. Moreover, the presence of Cu(I) can improve both the fluorescence intensity and the stability of DPA@CuNCs [37].

Fig. 1D shows the TEM images of DPA@CuNCs and the particle-size distribution of DPA@CuNCs (Inset of Fig. 1D). As shown in Fig. 1D, DPA@CuNCs emerged as spherical particles with diameters of approximately 40 nm. Moreover, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1D, the particle-size distribution of the samples was good. These results indicated that DPA@CuNCs were successfully synthesized while DPA was used as both a reducing agent and a protecting agent.

Fig. 2 shows the fluorescence properties of samples under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 2A, the stock solution of DPA@CuNCs was light yellow under sunlight and emitted a strong red fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm). The diluted solution of DPA@CuNCs was colorless under sunlight and could also emit strong red fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp. Fig. 2B shows the excitation spectra and emission spectra of DPA@CuNCs. The best excitation wavelength was 280 nm and the maximum emission wavelength was 640 nm, which was in line with the previously published paper [38]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2C, under different excitation wavelengths, the maximum emission wavelength of DPA@CuNCs hardly shifted, indicating that DPA@CuNCs had no excitation wavelength dependency [39]. Fig. 2D shows the emission spectra of DPA@CuNCs with different ratios of DPA to Cu(NO3)2·3H2O used during preparation. As shown in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence intensity of the samples was the highest when the ratio of DPA to Cu (NO3)2·3H2O was 5:1. Fig. 2E and F show the emission spectra of DPA@CuNCs under different reaction temperatures (E) and different reaction times (F). As shown in Fig. 2E and F, the reaction temperature (from 20 to 27 ℃) had little effect on the fluorescence intensities of the samples, and the best reaction time was 30 min. Moreover, DPA@CuNCs had good fluorescence stability, which was discussed in the Supplementary Information (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC and quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of pH and incubation time on the fluorescence of DPA@CuNCs were investigated in advance, which could be found in the Supplementary Information (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S2, at pH = 5 or at 20 min of incubation time, the quenching effects of OTC reached the maximum. Moreover, Fig. 3 A and B show the fluorescence emission spectra of DPA@CuNC in the presence of OTC (A), and the linear plot of the F0-F values versus the OTC concentrations (B). As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of DPA@CuNCs decreased with the increase in OTC concentrations. A linear plot of the F0-F values versus the OTC concentrations (from 5 to 60 μmol/L) was obtained (Fig. 3B), with the corresponding linear regression equation of Fi = 5.7421 Ci + 2.8363 (R 2 = 0.9983). In addition, the limit of detection (LOD, 3 σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of three blank controls and k is the slope of the regression equation) could reach 0.026 μ mol/L. These results indicated that the DPA@CuNCs-based probe can be used to detect OTC in the environment.

To investigate the quenching mechanism of OTC to DPA@CuNCs, the UV visible absorption spectra and the fluorescence lifetime of the samples were measured. Fig. 3C shows the UV–vis spectra of DPA@CuNCs, DPA@CuNCs in the presence of OTC, and OTC. As shown in Fig. 3C, OTC had an absorption peak between 250 nm and 400 nm, especially the absorption peak near 275 nm that was overlapped with the optimum excitation wavelength (280 nm) of DPA@CuNCs. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of DPA@CuNCs might be caused by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or fluorescence inner filter effect (IFE) [40]. However, as shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence lifetime of DPA@CuNCs in the absence of OTC was 6.1 ns, and that of DPA@CuNCs in the presence of OTC was also 6.1 ns, so both of them had the same value. This indicated that there was no electron transfer between DPA@CuNCs and OTC [41], and the FRET mechanism could be excluded. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of DPA@CuNCs was caused by the IFE mechanism.

Fig. 4 shows the influence of interference ions (A) and organic compounds (B) on the F0-F values of DPA@CuNCs. As shown in Fig. 4A, the interference ions, such as K+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Eu3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, and Pb2+ showed weak fluorescence quenching effects on the DPA@CuNCs. Similarly, the interference organic compounds, such as streptomycin, sodium artemisinin, sodium penicillin, lincomycin hydrochloride, atropine citrulline, glutamate, ascorbic acid, cysteine, and hemoglobin, showed weak fluorescence quenching effects on the DPA@CuNCs (Fig. 4B). Moreover, as shown in Fig. S3, the interference experiments when the detection of OTC was performed in the presence of these interferences indicated that these interferences had little impact on the detection of OTC.

However, we evaluated the effect of other antibiotics such as tetracycline (TC), chlortetracycline (CTC), and doxycycline (DOC) on the detection of OTC in the supplementary information (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. S4, although OTC had the best fluorescence quenching effect on the DPA@CuNCs, other antibiotics also had a certain fluorescence quenching effect on the DPA@CuNCs, which might be related to the similar structures between these antibiotics and OTC. The results also indicated that the interference of other antibiotics should be excluded when OTC was detected by this method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC in real samples and comparison of this method with other methods
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application of the probe, DPA@CuNCs were used to detect OTC in swine wastewater and pig kidney extracts. Table 1 shows the detection results of OTC in swine wastewater and pig kidney extracts. As shown in Table 1, recoveries ranging from 100.8 to 102.1 % were obtained in the samples of swine wastewater, and those ranging from 101.8 to 104.8 % were obtained in the samples of pig kidney extracts, all with good RSD values. These results indicated that DPA@CuNCs were reliable probes that could be used to detect OTC in environmental and biological samples.

Moreover, the comparison of this method with other similar methods is shown in Table S2. Qi et al. [42] reported an aptamer-based colorimetric method for the detection of tetracycline (TC). Kim and coworkers [8] reported a similar colorimetric method for the detection of OTC by using the OTC binding DNA aptamers. However, they all used gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) as the probe, which was more expensive. Cai et al. [32] reported CuNCs as temperature sensors and “turn off” detection of OTC. However, they used polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a templating agent in the synthesis of CuNCs@PEI, which was more expensive than the DPA. Pérez-Silva et al. [6] applied polymer inclusion membrane separation coupled to HPLC for the detection of OTC in milk samples, which required more complicated operations and expensive instruments. Nevertheless, how to sensitively detect OTC in complex biological systems using this method is still a challenge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of paper-based sensor for visual detection of OTC
ce:section.ce:para: To detect OTC more conveniently and rapidly, simple and low-cost test papers for the visual detection of OTC were finally fabricated in this work. The filter papers were soaked into DPA@CuNCs solution for 30 min and were air-dried naturally to obtain visual paper-based sensors, and then they were stored in dark for further use. Fig. 5 A shows the photos of test papers stored in the dark for different days that were illuminated by sunlight (a) and by ultraviolet light (b). As shown in Fig. 5A, the colors of the test papers had not changed under sunlight. However, the colors of the test papers turned bright red under ultraviolet light. Moreover, after being stored for 20 days in the dark, the test papers could still emit bright red fluorescence under ultraviolet light, indicating that the test papers had good fluorescence stability. Fig. 5B shows the photos of test papers loaded with different concentrations of OTC under sunlight illumination (a) and ultraviolet light illumination (b). As shown in Fig. 5B, with the increase in OTC concentrations, the colors of the test papers had not changed under sunlight. However, with the increase in OTC concentrations, the colors of the test papers gradually changed from red to blue-purple. Therefore, the concentration range of OTC in the sample could be roughly determined by observing the color changes of the test papers with the naked eye under the ultraviolet lamp. The development of the test papers makes it possible to visually detect OTC more conveniently and quickly in complex environments.

In this work, a novel fluorescence probe based on DPA@CuNCs for sensitive detection of OTC was prepared by a one-step method. The probe has good fluorescence stability, and it could specifically detect OTC with a good linear relationship and a low LOD. Meanwhile, the probe was successfully applied to the detection of OTC in real samples, such as swine wastewater and pig kidney extracts, which showed good recoveries and satisfactory RSD values. Moreover, a simple and low-cost test paper based on DPA@CuNCs for the visual detection of OTC was successfully developed, which could detect OTC more conveniently and quickly in complex environments.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51263009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122286.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
